The problem involves two dormitories named Oakwood and Maplewood.
We are told that the total number of students in both dorms is ninety-six.
Initially, the number of students in each dorm was not the same.
Five students moved from Maplewood to Oakwood to equalize the populations.
After this move, both dorms have the exact same number of residents.
Since the total is ninety-six, each dorm must now hold forty-eight students.
This means Oakwood has forty-eight students after the relocation event.
Before the move, Oakwood had fewer students than it does now.
Specifically, five students were added to Oakwood to reach the final count.
Therefore, we must subtract five from the current number of forty-eight.
The calculation shows that Oakwood originally had forty-three students.
Maplewood must have started with a higher number of students initially.
If Oakwood had forty-three, then Maplewood had fifty-three before the move.
Adding forty-three and fifty-three gives us the total of ninety-six students.
When five leave Maplewood, it drops from fifty-three to forty-eight.
When five enter Oakwood, it rises from forty-three to forty-eight.
The numbers now match perfectly as the problem statement describes.
This confirms our mathematical reasoning is sound and accurate.
